does: This paper presents a unified framework for enhancing reliability in robotic systems that integrate large language models, addressing both security and safety concerns simultaneously. The approach combines prompt assembling, state management, and safety validation mechanisms to protect against prompt injection attacks while ensuring safe operation in complex environments. The authors evaluate their framework using both performance and security metrics on LLM-integrated mobile robots, demonstrating a 30.8% improvement in resilience against injection attacks and up to 325% improvement in complex adversarial environment settings compared to baseline approaches. The work includes both simulation experiments and physical robot demonstrations, with open-source code and interactive demos provided for reproducibility and accessibility.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of LLM integration in robotics and AI safety literature. It responds to recent research identifying prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM-based systems, extending earlier work on adversarial robustness in language models to the embodied AI domain. The safety validation mechanisms draw from established robot safety frameworks and formal verification approaches, while the prompt assembling techniques relate to prompt engineering and instruction-following research. The paper also connects to broader security concerns in autonomous systems and the emerging field of AI reliability, addressing gaps where prior work has often treated security and safety as separate concerns rather than unified challenges in deployed robotic systems.
enables: This framework enables more reliable deployment of LLM-powered robots in safety-critical real-world applications by providing practical mitigation strategies against both security threats and operational failures. The unified approach suggests future work could extend these validation mechanisms to other embodied AI systems beyond mobile robots, such as manipulators or multi-agent systems. The open-sourced framework and demonstration platform facilitate community research into LLM robustness in physical environments, potentially accelerating standardization of reliability testing for LLM-integrated systems. The work points toward developing formal verification methods for LLM-based decision-making and establishing comprehensive benchmarks for evaluating reliability in adversarial conditions, supporting the transition from laboratory settings to widespread industrial and autonomous system deployment.
